Yamasaki|Yamasaki Masatada]] before the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' [[honden]] in 1932/3]] The shrine is dedicated to [[Minamoto no Tametomo]], [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], [[:en:Shō En|Shō En]], [[:en:Shō Kei|Shō Kei]], [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]]. The first fought in the [[:en:Hōgen Rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] before making his way to Okinawa and siring there the future king [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], as told in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' and ''[[:en:Chūzan Seikan|Chūzan Seikan]]'', a tale that was exploited during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and after to help legitimize the annexation of the kingdom and its reconfiguration first as the [[:en:Ryūkyū Domain|Ryūkyū Domain]] and subsequently as [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]].{{cite book |title=Visions of Ryukyu |author=[[Gregory Smits|Smits, Gregory]] |publisher=[[University of Hawai'i Press]] |year=1999 |pages=58–62 |isbn=0-8248-2037-1}}{{cite book |title=Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan, 1879–2000 |author=Tze May Loo |publisher=[[Lexington Books]] |year=2014 |pages=93–97 |isbn=978-0-7391-8248-2}} The three [[Genealogy of the Shō Dynasties#Second Shō Dynasty|Shō Dynasty]] kings were, respectively, the founder of the dynasty, ruler during the kingdom's golden age, and its last king. ==Background== In 1889, the governor of Okinawa requested the establishment of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#National shrines, 2nd Rank|kokuhei chūsha]]'', not only "to cultivate the spirit of reverence and respect", but also on the grounds that this would be "invaluable in the government of the prefecture". [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] was proposed as the candidate. In 1910 it was proposed a new {{nihongo|prefectural shrine|[[:ja:県社|県社]]|kensha}} be established in honour of the fiftieth anniversary of the ascension to the [[:en:Chrysanthemum Throne|throne]] of the [[:en:Meiji emperor|Meiji emperor]]. Four years later there were similarly unsuccessful proposals to establish a prefectural shrine in the grounds of Naminoue-gū. In 1915 the prefectural government proposed to the [[:en:Home Ministry|Home Ministry]] the foundation of a shrine dedicated to [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]], [[Ryukyuan religion#Ryukyuan creation myth|Amamikyo and Shinerikyo]]; this was rejected on the grounds that the central figures of the [[Ryukyuan religion#Ryukyuan creation myth|Ryukyuan creation myth]] were not part of the [[Kami|Shinto pantheon]]. ==Establishment== In December 1922, the prefecture submitted another proposal for a prefectural shrine. With old [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]] as the location, the submission was approved by the Home Ministry on 31 March 1923. The main hall of the castle, then in a state of some disrepair, was to be demolished to make way for the [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]. Despite the appeal of [[:en:Higashionna Kanjun|Higashionna Kanjun]] to the mayor of [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]], work was already underway when {{nihongo|[[Kamakura Yoshitarō]]|[[:ja:鎌倉芳太郎|鎌倉芳太郎]]}} approached [[Itō Chūta]] to help save the site. The pair visited Okinawa together before the prominent architect, a member of the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1897 Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law|Committee for the Preservation of Ancient Shrines and Temples]], declaring the castle the "representative work of Ryūkyūan architecture", intervened to secure an emergency designation under the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1919 Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law|Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law]]. Demolition was halted. Itō proposed the main hall be used as the shrine haiden, thus qualifying it for funds for repair under the Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law. On 24 April 1925, as "Okinawa Jinja haiden", it was designated a [[National Treasure (Japan)#History|Specially Protected Building (National Treasure)]].{{cite web |url=http://www.museums.pref.okinawa.jp/museum/topics/detail.jsp?id=1528 |script-title=ja:「首里城を救った男」 |trans-title=The Man who Saved Shuri Castle |language=ja |publisher=[[Okinawa Prefectural Museum|Okinawa Prefectural Museum & Art Museum]] |date=1 March 2016 |accessdate=22 November 2016}} This ostensibly benign episode of heritage preservation might also be viewed as a [[:en:Postcolonialism|violent colonial "appropriation"]] of the palace of the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kings]], paradigmatic "marker of prior independence", for relocation within the "ideological universe" of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and service of the "[[:en:Empire of Japan|emperor-centred Japanese nation state]]". ==After 1945== [[File:Okinawa_Shrine2.JPG|thumb|left|250px|Okinawa Shrine on 11 September 2009]] The shrine and most of the rest of the castle were destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], and five years later, the [[:en:University of the Ryukyus|University of the Ryukyus]] was established on the former castle site in May 1950. Under [[:en:United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands|United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands]] Proclamation 16 (USCAR 16) of 1952, concerning land with uncertain ownership, the shrine grounds were transferred to the administration of [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri City]], which two years later was [[:en:Municipal mergers and dissolutions in Japan|merged]] into [[:en:Naha|Naha]].{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/site/somu/kanzai/zaicho/syoyushahumei/syoyuusyahumei.html |script-title=ja:所有者不明土地について |trans-title=Concerning land with uncertain ownership |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] |date=5 January 2016 |accessdate=26 November 2016}} In 1960, a proposal to rebuild the shrine on a plot to the east of the former castle main hall was vetoed by the University. The following year a temporary shrine was erected by the entrance to Bengadake, construction work continuing into 1962. In 1969 a tenancy agreement for the land occupied by the temporary shrine was agreed with Naha City by the Association for the Realization of the Reconstruction of Okinawa Shrine. As of 2016, Okinawa Shrine is one of eleven shrines overseen by the Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Agency, a branch of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].{{cite web |url=http://naminouegu.jp/english.html |title=Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Agency |publisher=[[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue-gū]] |accessdate=26 November 2016}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Okinawa-jinja}} * [[:en:Chōsen Jingū|Chōsen Jingū]] * [[:en:Taiwan Shrine|Taiwan Shrine]] * [[:en:Ryūkyū Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kingdom]] * [[:en:List of Cultural Properties of Japan - structures (Okinawa)|List of Cultural Properties of Japan - structures (Okinawa)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/okinawajinja.html Okinawa Jinja] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[en:Okinawa Shrine]] [[sn:Okinawa Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Okinawa-jinja}} [[id:Kuil Okinawa]] [[ja:沖縄神社]] [[zh:沖繩神社]]

沖縄神社 | image = Okinawa Jinja haiden.jpg | caption = Okinawa Shrine [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]], formerly the main hall of [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], before the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]; visible in the photo are the shrine [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]], [[:en:Stone lantern|Stone lantern]], [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]], and offering box | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|26|13|2.61|N|127|43|51.47|E|type:landmark_region:JP-47|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Minamoto no Tametomo]], [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], [[:en:Shō En|Shō En]], [[:en:Shō Kei|Shō Kei]], [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]]{{cite web |url=http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/okinawajinja.html |script-title=ja:沖縄神社 |trans-title=Okinawa Jinja |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue-gū]] |accessdate=26 November 2016}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 5 Shuritorihori-chō, [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = {{URL|http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/okinawajinja.html}} | architecture_style = | festival = 20 October | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Okinawa Shrine'''|沖縄神社|Okinawa Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. Established at the end of the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] on the site of [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], the main hall of which was reused as the [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]] (hall of worship), the shrine buildings were destroyed in May 1945 during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]].{{cite book |script-title=ja:戦災等による焼失文化財―20世紀の文化財過去帳 |trans-title=Cultural properties lost to fire during war etc: a register of former twentieth-century cultural properties |language=ja |editor=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |year=2003 |publisher=[[:ja:戎光祥出版|戎光祥出版]] |pages=487–8 |isbn=4-900901-34-2}} Both castle and shrine have since been rebuilt while this phase has been [[:en:Memory hole|written out]] of the "[[:en:official history|official history]]" currently told at Shurijō Castle Park.{{cite journal |url=http://apjjf.org/-Tze-M.-Loo/3232/article.html |title=Shuri Castle's Other History: Architecture and Empire in Okinawa |author=Tze May Loo |journal=[[The Asia-Pacific Journal: Japan Focus]] |year=2009 |volume=7 |number=41(1)}}{{cite book |title=Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan, 1879–2000 |author=Tze May Loo |publisher=[[Lexington Books]] |year=2014 |pages=55–57, 85–117 |isbn=978-0-7391-8248-2}}{{cite web |url=http://oki-park.jp/userfiles/files/pdf/shurijo/lang/history_en.pdf |title=History of Shurijo Castle |publisher=[[:en:Shuri Castle|Shurijo Castle Park]] |accessdate=24 November 2016}}{{cite web |url=http://oki-park.jp/shurijo/about/186 |script-title=ja:琉球王国とは |trans-title=About the Ryukyu Kingdom |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Shuri Castle|Shurijo Castle Park]] |accessdate=24 November 2016}} ==Dedication== [[File:Yamazaki Masatada at Okinawa Shrine.jpg|thumb|right|200px|[[:en:Masatada Yamasaki|Yamasaki Masatada]] before the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' [[honden]] in 1932/3]] The shrine is dedicated to [[Minamoto no Tametomo]], [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], [[:en:Shō En|Shō En]], [[:en:Shō Kei|Shō Kei]], [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]]. The first fought in the [[:en:Hōgen Rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] before making his way to Okinawa and siring there the future king [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], as told in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' and ''[[:en:Chūzan Seikan|Chūzan Seikan]]'', a tale that was exploited during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and after to help legitimize the annexation of the kingdom and its reconfiguration first as the [[:en:Ryūkyū Domain|Ryūkyū Domain]] and subsequently as [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]].{{cite book |title=Visions of Ryukyu |author=[[Gregory Smits|Smits, Gregory]] |publisher=[[University of Hawai'i Press]] |year=1999 |pages=58–62 |isbn=0-8248-2037-1}}{{cite book |title=Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan, 1879–2000 |author=Tze May Loo |publisher=[[Lexington Books]] |year=2014 |pages=93–97 |isbn=978-0-7391-8248-2}} The three [[Genealogy of the Shō Dynasties#Second Shō Dynasty|Shō Dynasty]] kings were, respectively, the founder of the dynasty, ruler during the kingdom's golden age, and its last king. ==Background== In 1889, the governor of Okinawa requested the establishment of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#National shrines, 2nd Rank|kokuhei chūsha]]'', not only "to cultivate the spirit of reverence and respect", but also on the grounds that this would be "invaluable in the government of the prefecture". Demolition was halted. Itō proposed the main hall be used as the shrine haiden, thus qualifying it for funds for repair under the Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law. On 24 April 1925, as "Okinawa Jinja haiden", it was designated a [[National Treasure (Japan)#History|Specially Protected Building (National Treasure)]].{{cite web |url=http://www.museums.pref.okinawa.jp/museum/topics/detail.jsp?id=1528 |script-title=ja:「首里城を救った男」 |trans-title=The Man who Saved Shuri Castle |language=ja |publisher=[[Okinawa Prefectural Museum|Okinawa Prefectural Museum & Art Museum]] |date=1 March 2016 |accessdate=22 November 2016}} This ostensibly benign episode of heritage preservation might also be viewed as a [[:en:Postcolonialism|violent colonial "appropriation"]] of the palace of the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kings]], paradigmatic "marker of prior independence", for relocation within the "ideological universe" of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and service of the "[[:en:Empire of Japan|emperor-centred Japanese nation state]]". ==After 1945== [[File:Okinawa_Shrine2.JPG|thumb|left|250px|Okinawa Shrine on 11 September 2009]] The shrine and most of the rest of the castle were destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], and five years later, the [[:en:University of the Ryukyus|University of the Ryukyus]] was established on the former castle site in May 1950. Under [[:en:United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands|United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands]] Proclamation 16 (USCAR 16) of 1952, concerning land with uncertain ownership, the shrine grounds were transferred to the administration of [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri City]], which two years later was [[:en:Municipal mergers and dissolutions in Japan|merged]] into [[:en:Naha|Naha]].{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/site/somu/kanzai/zaicho/syoyushahumei/syoyuusyahumei.html |script-title=ja:所有者不明土地について |trans-title=Concerning land with uncertain ownership |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] |date=5 January 2016 |accessdate=26 November 2016}} In 1960, a proposal to rebuild the shrine on a plot to the east of the former castle main hall was vetoed by the University. The following year a temporary shrine was erected by the entrance to Bengadake, construction work continuing into 1962. The pair visited Okinawa together before the prominent architect, a member of the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1897 Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law|Committee for the Preservation of Ancient Shrines and Temples]], declaring the castle the "representative work of Ryūkyūan architecture", intervened to secure an emergency designation under the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1919 Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law|Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law]]. Demolition was halted. Itō proposed the main hall be used as the shrine haiden, thus qualifying it for funds for repair under the Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law. On 24 April 1925, as "Okinawa Jinja haiden", it was designated a [[National Treasure (Japan)#History|Specially Protected Building (National Treasure)]].{{cite web |url=http://www.museums.pref.okinawa.jp/museum/topics/detail.jsp?id=1528 |script-title=ja:「首里城を救った男」 |trans-title=The Man who Saved Shuri Castle |language=ja |publisher=[[Okinawa Prefectural Museum|Okinawa Prefectural Museum & Art Museum]] |date=1 March 2016 |accessdate=22 November 2016}} This ostensibly benign episode of heritage preservation might also be viewed as a [[:en:Postcolonialism|violent colonial "appropriation"]] of the palace of the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kings]], paradigmatic "marker of prior independence", for relocation within the "ideological universe" of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and service of the "[[:en:Empire of Japan|emperor-centred Japanese nation state]]". ==After 1945== [[File:Okinawa_Shrine2.JPG|thumb|left|250px|Okinawa Shrine on 11 September 2009]] The shrine and most of the rest of the castle were destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], and five years later, the [[:en:University of the Ryukyus|University of the Ryukyus]] was established on the former castle site in May 1950. Under [[:en:United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands|United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands]] Proclamation 16 (USCAR 16) of 1952, concerning land with uncertain ownership, the shrine grounds were transferred to the administration of [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri City]], which two years later was [[:en:Municipal mergers and dissolutions in Japan|merged]] into [[:en:Naha|Naha]].{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/site/somu/kanzai/zaicho/syoyushahumei/syoyuusyahumei.html |script-title=ja:所有者不明土地について |trans-title=Concerning land with uncertain ownership |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] |date=5 January 2016 |accessdate=26 November 2016}} In 1960, a proposal to rebuild the shrine on a plot to the east of the former castle main hall was vetoed by the University. The following year a temporary shrine was erected by the entrance to Bengadake, construction work continuing into 1962. In 1969 a tenancy agreement for the land occupied by the temporary shrine was agreed with Naha City by the Association for the Realization of the Reconstruction of Okinawa Shrine. As of 2016, Okinawa Shrine is one of eleven shrines overseen by the Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Agency, a branch of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].{{cite web |url=http://naminouegu.jp/english.html |title=Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Agency |publisher=[[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue-gū]] |accessdate=26 November 2016}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Okinawa-jinja}} * [[:en:Chōsen Jingū|Chōsen Jingū]] * [[:en:Taiwan Shrine|Taiwan Shrine]] * [[:en:Ryūkyū Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kingdom]] * [[:en:List of Cultural Properties of Japan - structures (Okinawa)|List of Cultural Properties of Japan - structures (Okinawa)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/okinawajinja.html Okinawa Jinja] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[en:Okinawa Shrine]] [[sn:Okinawa Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Okinawa-jinja}} [[id:Kuil Okinawa]] [[ja:沖縄神社]] [[zh:沖繩神社]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11139392]] Wikidata name: Okinawa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okinawa Jinja
沖縄神社 | image = Okinawa Jinja haiden.jpg | caption = Okinawa Shrine [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]], formerly the main hall of [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], before the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]; visible in the photo are the shrine [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]], [[:en:Stone lantern|Stone lantern]], [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]], and offering box | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|26|13|2.61|N|127|43|51.47|E|type:landmark_region:JP-47|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Minamoto no Tametomo]], [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], [[:en:Shō En|Shō En]], [[:en:Shō Kei|Shō Kei]], [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]]{{cite web |url=http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/okinawajinja.html |script-title=ja:沖縄神社 |trans-title=Okinawa Jinja |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue-gū]] |accessdate=26 November 2016}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 5 Shuritorihori-chō, [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = {{URL|http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/okinawajinja.html}} | architecture_style = | festival = 20 October | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Okinawa Shrine'''|沖縄神社|Okinawa Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. Established at the end of the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] on the site of [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], the main hall of which was reused as the [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]] (hall of worship), the shrine buildings were destroyed in May 1945 during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]].{{cite book |script-title=ja:戦災等による焼失文化財―20世紀の文化財過去帳 |trans-title=Cultural properties lost to fire during war etc: a register of former twentieth-century cultural properties |language=ja |editor=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |year=2003 |publisher=[[:ja:戎光祥出版|戎光祥出版]] |pages=487–8 |isbn=4-900901-34-2}} Both castle and shrine have since been rebuilt while this phase has been [[:en:Memory hole|written out]] of the "[[:en:official history|official history]]" currently told at Shurijō Castle Park.{{cite journal |url=http://apjjf.org/-Tze-M.-Loo/3232/article.html |title=Shuri Castle's Other History: Architecture and Empire in Okinawa |author=Tze May Loo |journal=[[The Asia-Pacific Journal: Japan Focus]] |year=2009 |volume=7 |number=41(1)}}{{cite book |title=Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan, 1879–2000 |author=Tze May Loo |publisher=[[Lexington Books]] |year=2014 |pages=55–57, 85–117 |isbn=978-0-7391-8248-2}}{{cite web |url=http://oki-park.jp/userfiles/files/pdf/shurijo/lang/history_en.pdf |title=History of Shurijo Castle |publisher=[[:en:Shuri Castle|Shurijo Castle Park]] |accessdate=24 November 2016}}{{cite web |url=http://oki-park.jp/shurijo/about/186 |script-title=ja:琉球王国とは |trans-title=About the Ryukyu Kingdom |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Shuri Castle|Shurijo Castle Park]] |accessdate=24 November 2016}} ==Dedication== [[File:Yamazaki Masatada at Okinawa Shrine.jpg|thumb|right|200px|[[:en:Masatada Yamasaki|Yamasaki Masatada]] before the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' [[honden]] in 1932/3]] The shrine is dedicated to [[Minamoto no Tametomo]], [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], [[:en:Shō En|Shō En]], [[:en:Shō Kei|Shō Kei]], [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]]. The first fought in the [[:en:Hōgen Rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] before making his way to Okinawa and siring there the future king [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], as told in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' and ''[[:en:Chūzan Seikan|Chūzan Seikan]]'', a tale that was exploited during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and after to help legitimize the annexation of the kingdom and its reconfiguration first as the [[:en:Ryūkyū Domain|Ryūkyū Domain]] and subsequently as [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]].{{cite book |title=Visions of Ryukyu |author=[[Gregory Smits|Smits, Gregory]] |publisher=[[University of Hawai'i Press]] |year=1999 |pages=58–62 |isbn=0-8248-2037-1}}{{cite book |title=Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan, 1879–2000 |author=Tze May Loo |publisher=[[Lexington Books]] |year=2014 |pages=93–97 |isbn=978-0-7391-8248-2}} The three [[Genealogy of the Shō Dynasties#Second Shō Dynasty|Shō Dynasty]] kings were, respectively, the founder of the dynasty, ruler during the kingdom's golden age, and its last king. ==Background== In 1889, the governor of Okinawa requested the establishment of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#National shrines, 2nd Rank|kokuhei chūsha]]'', not only "to cultivate the spirit of reverence and respect", but also on the grounds that this would be "invaluable in the government of the prefecture". [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] was proposed as the candidate. In 1910 it was proposed a new {{nihongo|prefectural shrine|[[:ja:県社|県社]]|kensha}} be established in honour of the fiftieth anniversary of the ascension to the [[:en:Chrysanthemum Throne|throne]] of the [[:en:Meiji emperor|Meiji emperor]]. Four years later there were similarly unsuccessful proposals to establish a prefectural shrine in the grounds of Naminoue-gū. In 1915 the prefectural government proposed to the [[:en:Home Ministry|Home Ministry]] the foundation of a shrine dedicated to [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]], [[Ryukyuan religion#Ryukyuan creation myth|Amamikyo and Shinerikyo]]; this was rejected on the grounds that the central figures of the [[Ryukyuan religion#Ryukyuan creation myth|Ryukyuan creation myth]] were not part of the [[Kami|Shinto pantheon]]. ==Establishment== In December 1922, the prefecture submitted another proposal for a prefectural shrine. With old [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]] as the location, the submission was approved by the Home Ministry on 31 March 1923. The main hall of the castle, then in a state of some disrepair, was to be demolished to make way for the [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]. Despite the appeal of [[:en:Higashionna Kanjun|Higashionna Kanjun]] to the mayor of [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]], work was already underway when {{nihongo|[[Kamakura Yoshitarō]]|[[:ja:鎌倉芳太郎|鎌倉芳太郎]]}} approached [[Itō Chūta]] to help save the site. The pair visited Okinawa together before the prominent architect, a member of the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1897 Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law|Committee for the Preservation of Ancient Shrines and Temples]], declaring the castle the "representative work of Ryūkyūan architecture", intervened to secure an emergency designation under the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1919 Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law|Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law]]. Demolition was halted. Itō proposed the main hall be used as the shrine haiden, thus qualifying it for funds for repair under the Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law. On 24 April 1925, as "Okinawa Jinja haiden", it was designated a [[National Treasure (Japan)#History|Specially Protected Building (National Treasure)]].{{cite web |url=http://www.museums.pref.okinawa.jp/museum/topics/detail.jsp?id=1528 |script-title=ja:「首里城を救った男」 |trans-title=The Man who Saved Shuri Castle |language=ja |publisher=[[Okinawa Prefectural Museum|Okinawa Prefectural Museum & Art Museum]] |date=1 March 2016 |accessdate=22 November 2016}} This ostensibly benign episode of heritage preservation might also be viewed as a [[:en:Postcolonialism|violent colonial "appropriation"]] of the palace of the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kings]], paradigmatic "marker of prior independence", for relocation within the "ideological universe" of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and service of the "[[:en:Empire of Japan|emperor-centred Japanese nation state]]". ==After 1945== [[File:Okinawa_Shrine2.JPG|thumb|left|250px|Okinawa Shrine on 11 September 2009]] The shrine and most of the rest of the castle were destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], and five years later, the [[:en:University of the Ryukyus|University of the Ryukyus]] was established on the former castle site in May 1950. Under [[:en:United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands|United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands]] Proclamation 16 (USCAR 16) of 1952, concerning land with uncertain ownership, the shrine grounds were transferred to the administration of [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri City]], which two years later was [[:en:Municipal mergers and dissolutions in Japan|merged]] into [[:en:Naha|Naha]].{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/site/somu/kanzai/zaicho/syoyushahumei/syoyuusyahumei.html |script-title=ja:所有者不明土地について |trans-title=Concerning land with uncertain ownership |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] |date=5 January 2016 |accessdate=26 November 2016}} In 1960, a proposal to rebuild the shrine on a plot to the east of the former castle main hall was vetoed by the University. The following year a temporary shrine was erected by the entrance to Bengadake, construction work continuing into 1962. In 1969 a tenancy agreement for the land occupied by the temporary shrine was agreed with Naha City by the Association for the Realization of the Reconstruction of Okinawa Shrine. As of 2016, Okinawa Shrine is one of eleven shrines overseen by the Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Agency, a branch of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].{{cite web |url=http://naminouegu.jp/english.html |title=Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Agency |publisher=[[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue-gū]] |accessdate=26 November 2016}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Okinawa-jinja}} * [[:en:Chōsen Jingū|Chōsen Jingū]] * [[:en:Taiwan Shrine|Taiwan Shrine]] * [[:en:Ryūkyū Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kingdom]] * [[:en:List of Cultural Properties of Japan - structures (Okinawa)|List of Cultural Properties of Japan - structures (Okinawa)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/okinawajinja.html Okinawa Jinja] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[en:Okinawa Shrine]] [[sn:Okinawa Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Okinawa-jinja}} [[id:Kuil Okinawa]] [[ja:沖縄神社]] [[zh:沖繩神社]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11139392]] Wikidata name: Okinawa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okinawa Jinja
沖縄神社 | image = File:Okinawa Shrine remains.jpg | caption = Okinawa Shrine [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]], formerly the main hall of [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], before the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]; visible in the photo are the shrine [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]], [[:en:Stone lantern|Stone lantern]], [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]], and offering box | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|26.21738611|N|127.73093333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Shunten|qid=Q1059918}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 5 Shuritorihori-chō, [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = {{URL|http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/okinawajinja.html}} | architecture_style = | festival = 20 October | leadership = | location = {{ill|Naha|qid=Q181966}} }} {{nihongo|'''Okinawa Shrine'''|沖縄神社|Okinawa Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. Established at the end of the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] on the site of [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], the main hall of which was reused as the [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]] (hall of worship), the shrine buildings were destroyed in May 1945 during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]].{{cite book |script-title=ja:戦災等による焼失文化財―20世紀の文化財過去帳 |trans-title=Cultural properties lost to fire during war etc: a register of former twentieth-century cultural properties |language=ja |editor=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |year=2003 |publisher=[[:ja:戎光祥出版|戎光祥出版]] |pages=487–8 |isbn=4-900901-34-2}} Both castle and shrine have since been rebuilt while this phase has been [[:en:Memory hole|written out]] of the "[[:en:official history|official history]]" currently told at Shurijō Castle Park.{{cite journal |url=http://apjjf.org/-Tze-M.-Loo/3232/article.html |title=Shuri Castle's Other History: Architecture and Empire in Okinawa |author=Tze May Loo |journal=[[The Asia-Pacific Journal: Japan Focus]] |year=2009 |volume=7 |number=41(1)}}{{cite book |title=Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan, 1879–2000 |author=Tze May Loo |publisher=[[Lexington Books]] |year=2014 |pages=55–57, 85–117 |isbn=978-0-7391-8248-2}}{{cite web |url=http://oki-park.jp/userfiles/files/pdf/shurijo/lang/history_en.pdf |title=History of Shurijo Castle |publisher=[[:en:Shuri Castle|Shurijo Castle Park]] |accessdate=24 November 2016}}{{cite web |url=http://oki-park.jp/shurijo/about/186 |script-title=ja:琉球王国とは |trans-title=About the Ryukyu Kingdom |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Shuri Castle|Shurijo Castle Park]] |accessdate=24 November 2016}} ==Dedication== [[File:Yamazaki Masatada at Okinawa Shrine.jpg|thumb|right|200px|[[:en:Masatada Yamasaki|Yamasaki Masatada]] before the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' [[honden]] in 1932/3]] The shrine is dedicated to [[Minamoto no Tametomo]], [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], [[:en:Shō En|Shō En]], [[:en:Shō Kei|Shō Kei]], [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]]. The first fought in the [[:en:Hōgen Rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] before making his way to Okinawa and siring there the future king [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], as told in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' and ''[[:en:Chūzan Seikan|Chūzan Seikan]]'', a tale that was exploited during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and after to help legitimize the annexation of the kingdom and its reconfiguration first as the [[:en:Ryūkyū Domain|Ryūkyū Domain]] and subsequently as [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]].{{cite book |title=Visions of Ryukyu |author=[[Gregory Smits|Smits, Gregory]] |publisher=[[University of Hawai'i Press]] |year=1999 |pages=58–62 |isbn=0-8248-2037-1}}{{cite book |title=Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan, 1879–2000 |author=Tze May Loo |publisher=[[Lexington Books]] |year=2014 |pages=93–97 |isbn=978-0-7391-8248-2}} The three [[Genealogy of the Shō Dynasties#Second Shō Dynasty|Shō Dynasty]] kings were, respectively, the founder of the dynasty, ruler during the kingdom's golden age, and its last king. ==Background== In 1889, the governor of Okinawa requested the establishment of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#National shrines, 2nd Rank|kokuhei chūsha]]'', not only "to cultivate the spirit of reverence and respect", but also on the grounds that this would be "invaluable in the government of the prefecture". [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] was proposed as the candidate. In 1910 it was proposed a new {{nihongo|prefectural shrine|[[:ja:県社|県社]]|kensha}} be established in honour of the fiftieth anniversary of the ascension to the [[:en:Chrysanthemum Throne|throne]] of the [[:en:Meiji emperor|Meiji emperor]]. Four years later there were similarly unsuccessful proposals to establish a prefectural shrine in the grounds of Naminoue-gū. In 1915 the prefectural government proposed to the [[:en:Home Ministry|Home Ministry]] the foundation of a shrine dedicated to [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]], [[Ryukyuan religion#Ryukyuan creation myth|Amamikyo and Shinerikyo]]; this was rejected on the grounds that the central figures of the [[Ryukyuan religion#Ryukyuan creation myth|Ryukyuan creation myth]] were not part of the [[Kami|Shinto pantheon]]. ==Establishment== In December 1922, the prefecture submitted another proposal for a prefectural shrine. With old [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]] as the location, the submission was approved by the Home Ministry on 31 March 1923. The main hall of the castle, then in a state of some disrepair, was to be demolished to make way for the [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]. Despite the appeal of [[:en:Higashionna Kanjun|Higashionna Kanjun]] to the mayor of [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]], work was already underway when {{nihongo|[[Kamakura Yoshitarō]]|[[:ja:鎌倉芳太郎|鎌倉芳太郎]]}} approached [[Itō Chūta]] to help save the site. The pair visited Okinawa together before the prominent architect, a member of the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1897 Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law|Committee for the Preservation of Ancient Shrines and Temples]], declaring the castle the "representative work of Ryūkyūan architecture", intervened to secure an emergency designation under the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1919 Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law|Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law]]. Demolition was halted. Itō proposed the main hall be used as the shrine haiden, thus qualifying it for funds for repair under the Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law. On 24 April 1925, as "Okinawa Jinja haiden", it was designated a [[National Treasure (Japan)#History|Specially Protected Building (National Treasure)]].{{cite web |url=http://www.museums.pref.okinawa.jp/museum/topics/detail.jsp?id=1528 |script-title=ja:「首里城を救った男」 |trans-title=The Man who Saved Shuri Castle |language=ja |publisher=[[Okinawa Prefectural Museum|Okinawa Prefectural Museum & Art Museum]] |date=1 March 2016 |accessdate=22 November 2016}} This ostensibly benign episode of heritage preservation might also be viewed as a [[:en:Postcolonialism|violent colonial "appropriation"]] of the palace of the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kings]], paradigmatic "marker of prior independence", for relocation within the "ideological universe" of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and service of the "[[:en:Empire of Japan|emperor-centred Japanese nation state]]". ==After 1945== [[File:Okinawa_Shrine2.JPG|thumb|left|250px|Okinawa Shrine on 11 September 2009]] The shrine and most of the rest of the castle were destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], and five years later, the [[:en:University of the Ryukyus|University of the Ryukyus]] was established on the former castle site in May 1950. Under [[:en:United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands|United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands]] Proclamation 16 (USCAR 16) of 1952, concerning land with uncertain ownership, the shrine grounds were transferred to the administration of [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri City]], which two years later was [[:en:Municipal mergers and dissolutions in Japan|merged]] into [[:en:Naha|Naha]].{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/site/somu/kanzai/zaicho/syoyushahumei/syoyuusyahumei.html |script-title=ja:所有者不明土地について |trans-title=Concerning land with uncertain ownership |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] |date=5 January 2016 |accessdate=26 November 2016}} In 1960, a proposal to rebuild the shrine on a plot to the east of the former castle main hall was vetoed by the University. The following year a temporary shrine was erected by the entrance to Bengadake, construction work continuing into 1962. In 1969 a tenancy agreement for the land occupied by the temporary shrine was agreed with Naha City by the Association for the Realization of the Reconstruction of Okinawa Shrine. As of 2016, Okinawa Shrine is one of eleven shrines overseen by the Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Agency, a branch of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].{{cite web |url=http://naminouegu.jp/english.html |title=Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Agency |publisher=[[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue-gū]] |accessdate=26 November 2016}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Okinawa-jinja}} * [[:en:Chōsen Jingū|Chōsen Jingū]] * [[:en:Taiwan Shrine|Taiwan Shrine]] * [[:en:Ryūkyū Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kingdom]] * [[:en:List of Cultural Properties of Japan - structures (Okinawa)|List of Cultural Properties of Japan - structures (Okinawa)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/okinawajinja.html Okinawa Jinja] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[en:Okinawa Shrine]] [[sn:Okinawa Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Okinawa-jinja}} [[id:Kuil Okinawa]] [[ja:沖縄神社]] [[zh:沖繩神社]]

Kei|Shō Kei]], [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]]. The first fought in the [[:en:Hōgen Rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] before making his way to Okinawa and siring there the future king [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], as told in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' and ''[[:en:Chūzan Seikan|Chūzan Seikan]]'', a tale that was exploited during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and after to help legitimize the annexation of the kingdom and its reconfiguration first as the [[:en:Ryūkyū Domain|Ryūkyū Domain]] and subsequently as [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]].{{cite book |title=Visions of Ryukyu |author=[[Gregory Smits|Smits, Gregory]] |publisher=[[University of Hawai'i Press]] |year=1999 |pages=58–62 |isbn=0-8248-2037-1}}{{cite book |title=Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan, 1879–2000 |author=Tze May Loo |publisher=[[Lexington Books]] |year=2014 |pages=93–97 |isbn=978-0-7391-8248-2}} The three [[Genealogy of the Shō Dynasties#Second Shō Dynasty|Shō Dynasty]] kings were, respectively, the founder of the dynasty, ruler during the kingdom's golden age, and its last king. ==Background== In 1889, the governor of Okinawa requested the establishment of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#National shrines, 2nd Rank|kokuhei chūsha]]'', not only "to cultivate the spirit of reverence and respect", but also on the grounds that this would be "invaluable in the government of the prefecture". [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] was proposed as the candidate. In 1910 it was proposed a new {{nihongo|prefectural shrine|[[:ja:県社|県社]]|kensha}} be established in honour of the fiftieth anniversary of the ascension to the [[:en:Chrysanthemum Throne|throne]] of the [[:en:Meiji emperor|Meiji emperor]]. Four years later there were similarly unsuccessful proposals to establish a prefectural shrine in the grounds of Naminoue-gū. In 1915 the prefectural government proposed to the [[:en:Home Ministry|Home Ministry]] the foundation of a shrine dedicated to [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]], [[Ryukyuan religion#Ryukyuan creation myth|Amamikyo and Shinerikyo]]; this was rejected on the grounds that the central figures of the [[Ryukyuan religion#Ryukyuan creation myth|Ryukyuan creation myth]] were not part of the [[Kami|Shinto pantheon]]. ==Establishment== In December 1922, the prefecture submitted another proposal for a prefectural shrine. With old [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]] as the location, the submission was approved by the Home Ministry on 31 March 1923. The main hall of the castle, then in a state of some disrepair, was to be demolished to make way for the [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]. Despite the appeal of [[:en:Higashionna Kanjun|Higashionna Kanjun]] to the mayor of [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]], work was already underway when {{nihongo|[[Kamakura Yoshitarō]]|[[:ja:鎌倉芳太郎|鎌倉芳太郎]]}} approached [[Itō Chūta]] to help save the site. The pair visited Okinawa together before the prominent architect, a member of the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1897 Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law|Committee for the Preservation of Ancient Shrines and Temples]], declaring the castle the "representative work of Ryūkyūan architecture", intervened to secure an emergency designation under the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1919 Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law|Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law]]. Demolition was halted. Itō proposed the main hall be used as the shrine haiden, thus qualifying it for funds for repair under the Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law. On 24 April 1925, as "Okinawa Jinja haiden", it was designated a [[National Treasure (Japan)#History|Specially Protected Building (National Treasure)]].{{cite web |url=http://www.museums.pref.okinawa.jp/museum/topics/detail.jsp?id=1528 |script-title=ja:「首里城を救った男」 |trans-title=The Man who Saved Shuri Castle |language=ja |publisher=[[Okinawa Prefectural Museum|Okinawa Prefectural Museum & Art Museum]] |date=1 March 2016 |accessdate=22 November 2016}} This ostensibly benign episode of heritage preservation might also be viewed as a [[:en:Postcolonialism|violent colonial "appropriation"]] of the palace of the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kings]], paradigmatic "marker of prior independence", for relocation within the "ideological universe" of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and service of the "[[:en:Empire of Japan|emperor-centred Japanese nation state]]". ==After 1945== [[File:Okinawa_Shrine2.JPG|thumb|left|250px|Okinawa Shrine on 11 September 2009]] The shrine and most of the rest of the castle were destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], and five years later, the [[:en:University of the Ryukyus|University of the Ryukyus]] was established on the former castle site in May 1950. Under [[:en:United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands|United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands]] Proclamation 16 (USCAR 16) of 1952, concerning land with uncertain ownership, the shrine grounds were transferred to the administration of [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri City]], which two years later was [[:en:Municipal mergers and dissolutions in Japan|merged]] into [[:en:Naha|Naha]].{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/site/somu/kanzai/zaicho/syoyushahumei/syoyuusyahumei.html |script-title=ja:所有者不明土地について |trans-title=Concerning land with uncertain ownership |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] |date=5 January 2016 |accessdate=26 November 2016}} In 1960, a proposal to rebuild the shrine on a plot to the east of the former castle main hall was vetoed by the University. The following year a temporary shrine was erected by the entrance to Bengadake, construction work continuing into 1962. In 1969 a tenancy agreement for the land occupied by the temporary shrine was agreed with Naha City by the Association for the Realization of the Reconstruction of Okinawa Shrine. As of 2016, Okinawa Shrine is one of eleven shrines overseen by the Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Agency, a branch of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].{{cite web |url=http://naminouegu.jp/english.html |title=Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Agency |publisher=[[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue-gū]] |accessdate=26 November 2016}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Okinawa-jinja}} * [[:en:Chōsen Jingū|Chōsen Jingū]] * [[:en:Taiwan Shrine|Taiwan Shrine]] * [[:en:Ryūkyū Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kingdom]] * [[:en:List of Cultural Properties of Japan - structures (Okinawa)|List of Cultural Properties of Japan - structures (Okinawa)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/okinawajinja.html Okinawa Jinja] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[en:Okinawa Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Okinawa-jinja}} [[id:Kuil Okinawa]] [[ja:沖縄神社]] [[zh:沖繩神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11139392}}

[[commons:Category:Okinawa-jinja]][[en:Okinawa Shrine]][[id:Kuil Okinawa]][[ja:沖縄神社]][[zh:沖繩神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11139392]] Wikidata name: Okinawa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okinawa Jinja
沖縄神社 | image = File:Okinawa Shrine remains.jpg | caption = Okinawa Shrine [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]], formerly the main hall of [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], before the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]; visible in the photo are the shrine [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]], [[:en:Stone lantern|Stone lantern]], [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]], and offering box | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|26.21738611|N|127.73093333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Shunten|qid=Q1059918}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 5 Shuritorihori-chō, [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = {{URL|http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/okinawajinja.html}} | architecture_style = | festival = 20 October | leadership = | location = {{ill|Naha|qid=Q181966}} }} {{nihongo|'''Okinawa Shrine'''|沖縄神社|Okinawa Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. Established at the end of the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] on the site of [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], the main hall of which was reused as the [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]] (hall of worship), the shrine buildings were destroyed in May 1945 during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]].{{cite book |script-title=ja:戦災等による焼失文化財―20世紀の文化財過去帳 |trans-title=Cultural properties lost to fire during war etc: a register of former twentieth-century cultural properties |language=ja |editor=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |year=2003 |publisher=[[:ja:戎光祥出版|戎光祥出版]] |pages=487–8 |isbn=4-900901-34-2}} Both castle and shrine have since been rebuilt while this phase has been [[:en:Memory hole|written out]] of the "[[:en:official history|official history]]" currently told at Shurijō Castle Park.{{cite journal |url=http://apjjf.org/-Tze-M.-Loo/3232/article.html |title=Shuri Castle's Other History: Architecture and Empire in Okinawa |author=Tze May Loo |journal=[[The Asia-Pacific Journal: Japan Focus]] |year=2009 |volume=7 |number=41(1)}}{{cite book |title=Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan, 1879–2000 |author=Tze May Loo |publisher=[[Lexington Books]] |year=2014 |pages=55–57, 85–117 |isbn=978-0-7391-8248-2}}{{cite web |url=http://oki-park.jp/userfiles/files/pdf/shurijo/lang/history_en.pdf |title=History of Shurijo Castle |publisher=[[:en:Shuri Castle|Shurijo Castle Park]] |accessdate=24 November 2016}}{{cite web |url=http://oki-park.jp/shurijo/about/186 |script-title=ja:琉球王国とは |trans-title=About the Ryukyu Kingdom |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Shuri Castle|Shurijo Castle Park]] |accessdate=24 November 2016}} ==Dedication== [[File:Yamazaki Masatada at Okinawa Shrine.jpg|thumb|right|200px|[[:en:Masatada Yamasaki|Yamasaki Masatada]] before the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' [[honden]] in 1932/3]] The shrine is dedicated to [[Minamoto no Tametomo]], [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], [[:en:Shō En|Shō En]], [[:en:Shō Kei|Shō Kei]], [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]]. The first fought in the [[:en:Hōgen Rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] before making his way to Okinawa and siring there the future king [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], as told in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' and ''[[:en:Chūzan Seikan|Chūzan Seikan]]'', a tale that was exploited during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and after to help legitimize the annexation of the kingdom and its reconfiguration first as the [[:en:Ryūkyū Domain|Ryūkyū Domain]] and subsequently as [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]].{{cite book |title=Visions of Ryukyu |author=[[Gregory Smits|Smits, Gregory]] |publisher=[[University of Hawai'i Press]] |year=1999 |pages=58–62 |isbn=0-8248-2037-1}}{{cite book |title=Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan, 1879–2000 |author=Tze May Loo |publisher=[[Lexington Books]] |year=2014 |pages=93–97 |isbn=978-0-7391-8248-2}} The three [[Genealogy of the Shō Dynasties#Second Shō Dynasty|Shō Dynasty]] kings were, respectively, the founder of the dynasty, ruler during the kingdom's golden age, and its last king. ==Background== In 1889, the governor of Okinawa requested the establishment of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#National shrines, 2nd Rank|kokuhei chūsha]]'', not only "to cultivate the spirit of reverence and respect", but also on the grounds that this would be "invaluable in the government of the prefecture". In 1915 the prefectural government proposed to the [[:en:Home Ministry|Home Ministry]] the foundation of a shrine dedicated to [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]], [[Ryukyuan religion#Ryukyuan creation myth|Amamikyo and Shinerikyo]]; this was rejected on the grounds that the central figures of the [[Ryukyuan religion#Ryukyuan creation myth|Ryukyuan creation myth]] were not part of the [[Kami|Shinto pantheon]]. ==Establishment== In December 1922, the prefecture submitted another proposal for a prefectural shrine. The pair visited Okinawa together before the prominent architect, a member of the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1897 Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law|Committee for the Preservation of Ancient Shrines and Temples]], declaring the castle the "representative work of Ryūkyūan architecture", intervened to secure an emergency designation under the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1919 Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law|Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law]]. Demolition was halted. Itō proposed the main hall be used as the shrine haiden, thus qualifying it for funds for repair under the Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law. On 24 April 1925, as "Okinawa Jinja haiden", it was designated a [[National Treasure (Japan)#History|Specially Protected Building (National Treasure)]].{{cite web |url=http://www.museums.pref.okinawa.jp/museum/topics/detail.jsp?id=1528 |script-title=ja:「首里城を救った男」 |trans-title=The Man who Saved Shuri Castle |language=ja |publisher=[[Okinawa Prefectural Museum|Okinawa Prefectural Museum & Art Museum]] |date=1 March 2016 |accessdate=22 November 2016}} This ostensibly benign episode of heritage preservation might also be viewed as a [[:en:Postcolonialism|violent colonial "appropriation"]] of the palace of the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kings]], paradigmatic "marker of prior independence", for relocation within the "ideological universe" of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and service of the "[[:en:Empire of Japan|emperor-centred Japanese nation state]]". ==After 1945== [[File:Okinawa_Shrine2.JPG|thumb|left|250px|Okinawa Shrine on 11 September 2009]] The shrine and most of the rest of the castle were destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], and five years later, the [[:en:University of the Ryukyus|University of the Ryukyus]] was established on the former castle site in May 1950. Under [[:en:United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands|United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands]] Proclamation 16 (USCAR 16) of 1952, concerning land with uncertain ownership, the shrine grounds were transferred to the administration of [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri City]], which two years later was [[:en:Municipal mergers and dissolutions in Japan|merged]] into [[:en:Naha|Naha]].{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/site/somu/kanzai/zaicho/syoyushahumei/syoyuusyahumei.html |script-title=ja:所有者不明土地について |trans-title=Concerning land with uncertain ownership |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] |date=5 January 2016 |accessdate=26 November 2016}} In 1960, a proposal to rebuild the shrine on a plot to the east of the former castle main hall was vetoed by the University. The pair visited Okinawa together before the prominent architect, a member of the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1897 Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law|Committee for the Preservation of Ancient Shrines and Temples]], declaring the castle the "representative work of Ryūkyūan architecture", intervened to secure an emergency designation under the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1919 Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law|Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law]]. Demolition was halted. Itō proposed the main hall be used as the shrine haiden, thus qualifying it for funds for repair under the Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law. On 24 April 1925, as "Okinawa Jinja haiden", it was designated a [[National Treasure (Japan)#History|Specially Protected Building (National Treasure)]].{{cite web |url=http://www.museums.pref.okinawa.jp/museum/topics/detail.jsp?id=1528 |script-title=ja:「首里城を救った男」 |trans-title=The Man who Saved Shuri Castle |language=ja |publisher=[[Okinawa Prefectural Museum|Okinawa Prefectural Museum & Art Museum]] |date=1 March 2016 |accessdate=22 November 2016}} This ostensibly benign episode of heritage preservation might also be viewed as a [[:en:Postcolonialism|violent colonial "appropriation"]] of the palace of the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kings]], paradigmatic "marker of prior independence", for relocation within the "ideological universe" of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and service of the "[[:en:Empire of Japan|emperor-centred Japanese nation state]]". ==After 1945== [[File:Okinawa_Shrine2.JPG|thumb|left|250px|Okinawa Shrine on 11 September 2009]] The shrine and most of the rest of the castle were destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], and five years later, the [[:en:University of the Ryukyus|University of the Ryukyus]] was established on the former castle site in May 1950. Under [[:en:United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands|United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands]] Proclamation 16 (USCAR 16) of 1952, concerning land with uncertain ownership, the shrine grounds were transferred to the administration of [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri City]], which two years later was [[:en:Municipal mergers and dissolutions in Japan|merged]] into [[:en:Naha|Naha]].{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/site/somu/kanzai/zaicho/syoyushahumei/syoyuusyahumei.html |script-title=ja:所有者不明土地について |trans-title=Concerning land with uncertain ownership |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] |date=5 January 2016 |accessdate=26 November 2016}} In 1960, a proposal to rebuild the shrine on a plot to the east of the former castle main hall was vetoed by the University. The following year a temporary shrine was erected by the entrance to Bengadake, construction work continuing into 1962. In 1969 a tenancy agreement for the land occupied by the temporary shrine was agreed with Naha City by the Association for the Realization of the Reconstruction of Okinawa Shrine. As of 2016, Okinawa Shrine is one of eleven shrines overseen by the Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Agency, a branch of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].{{cite web |url=http://naminouegu.jp/english.html |title=Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Agency |publisher=[[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue-gū]] |accessdate=26 November 2016}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Okinawa-jinja}} * [[:en:Chōsen Jingū|Chōsen Jingū]] * [[:en:Taiwan Shrine|Taiwan Shrine]] * [[:en:Ryūkyū Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kingdom]] * [[:en:List of Cultural Properties of Japan - structures (Okinawa)|List of Cultural Properties of Japan - structures (Okinawa)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/okinawajinja.html Okinawa Jinja] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[en:Okinawa Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Okinawa-jinja}} [[id:Kuil Okinawa]] [[ja:沖縄神社]] [[zh:沖繩神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11139392}}

revision.text:
[[sn:commons:Category:Okinawa-jinja]][[en:Okinawa Shrine]][[id:Kuil Okinawa]][[ja:沖縄神社]][[zh:沖繩神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11139392]] Wikidata name: Okinawa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okinawa Jinja
沖縄神社 | image = File:Okinawa Shrine remains.jpg | caption = Okinawa Shrine [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]], formerly the main hall of [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], before the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]; visible in the photo are the shrine [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]], [[:en:Stone lantern|Stone lantern]], [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]], and offering box | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|26.21738611|N|127.73093333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Shunten|WD=Q1059918}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 5 Shuritorihori-chō, [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = {{URL|http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/okinawajinja.html}} | architecture_style = | festival = 20 October | leadership = | location = {{ill|Naha|WD=Q181966}} }} {{nihongo|'''Okinawa Shrine'''|沖縄神社|Okinawa Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. Established at the end of the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] on the site of [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], the main hall of which was reused as the [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]] (hall of worship), the shrine buildings were destroyed in May 1945 during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]].{{cite book |script-title=ja:戦災等による焼失文化財―20世紀の文化財過去帳 |trans-title=Cultural properties lost to fire during war etc: a register of former twentieth-century cultural properties |language=ja |editor=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |year=2003 |publisher=[[:ja:戎光祥出版|戎光祥出版]] |pages=487–8 |isbn=4-900901-34-2}} Both castle and shrine have since been rebuilt while this phase has been [[:en:Memory hole|written out]] of the "[[:en:official history|official history]]" currently told at Shurijō Castle Park.{{cite journal |url=http://apjjf.org/-Tze-M.-Loo/3232/article.html |title=Shuri Castle's Other History: Architecture and Empire in Okinawa |author=Tze May Loo |journal=[[The Asia-Pacific Journal: Japan Focus]] |year=2009 |volume=7 |number=41(1)}}{{cite book |title=Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan, 1879–2000 |author=Tze May Loo |publisher=[[Lexington Books]] |year=2014 |pages=55–57, 85–117 |isbn=978-0-7391-8248-2}}{{cite web |url=http://oki-park.jp/userfiles/files/pdf/shurijo/lang/history_en.pdf |title=History of Shurijo Castle |publisher=[[:en:Shuri Castle|Shurijo Castle Park]] |accessdate=24 November 2016}}{{cite web |url=http://oki-park.jp/shurijo/about/186 |script-title=ja:琉球王国とは |trans-title=About the Ryukyu Kingdom |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Shuri Castle|Shurijo Castle Park]] |accessdate=24 November 2016}} ==Dedication== [[File:Yamazaki Masatada at Okinawa Shrine.jpg|thumb|right|200px|[[:en:Masatada Yamasaki|Yamasaki Masatada]] before the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' [[honden]] in 1932/3]] The shrine is dedicated to [[Minamoto no Tametomo]], [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], [[:en:Shō En|Shō En]], [[:en:Shō Kei|Shō Kei]], [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]]. The first fought in the [[:en:Hōgen Rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] before making his way to Okinawa and siring there the future king [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], as told in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' and ''[[:en:Chūzan Seikan|Chūzan Seikan]]'', a tale that was exploited during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and after to help legitimize the annexation of the kingdom and its reconfiguration first as the [[:en:Ryūkyū Domain|Ryūkyū Domain]] and subsequently as [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]].{{cite book |title=Visions of Ryukyu |author=[[Gregory Smits|Smits, Gregory]] |publisher=[[University of Hawai'i Press]] |year=1999 |pages=58–62 |isbn=0-8248-2037-1}}{{cite book |title=Heritage Politics: Shuri Castle and Okinawa's Incorporation into Modern Japan, 1879–2000 |author=Tze May Loo |publisher=[[Lexington Books]] |year=2014 |pages=93–97 |isbn=978-0-7391-8248-2}} The three [[Genealogy of the Shō Dynasties#Second Shō Dynasty|Shō Dynasty]] kings were, respectively, the founder of the dynasty, ruler during the kingdom's golden age, and its last king. ==Background== In 1889, the governor of Okinawa requested the establishment of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#National shrines, 2nd Rank|kokuhei chūsha]]'', not only "to cultivate the spirit of reverence and respect", but also on the grounds that this would be "invaluable in the government of the prefecture". [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] was proposed as the candidate. In 1910 it was proposed a new {{nihongo|prefectural shrine|[[:ja:県社|県社]]|kensha}} be established in honour of the fiftieth anniversary of the ascension to the [[:en:Chrysanthemum Throne|throne]] of the [[:en:Meiji emperor|Meiji emperor]]. Four years later there were similarly unsuccessful proposals to establish a prefectural shrine in the grounds of Naminoue-gū. In 1915 the prefectural government proposed to the [[:en:Home Ministry|Home Ministry]] the foundation of a shrine dedicated to [[:en:Shō Tai|Shō Tai]], [[Ryukyuan religion#Ryukyuan creation myth|Amamikyo and Shinerikyo]]; this was rejected on the grounds that the central figures of the [[Ryukyuan religion#Ryukyuan creation myth|Ryukyuan creation myth]] were not part of the [[Kami|Shinto pantheon]]. ==Establishment== In December 1922, the prefecture submitted another proposal for a prefectural shrine. With old [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]] as the location, the submission was approved by the Home Ministry on 31 March 1923. The main hall of the castle, then in a state of some disrepair, was to be demolished to make way for the [[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]. Despite the appeal of [[:en:Higashionna Kanjun|Higashionna Kanjun]] to the mayor of [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]], work was already underway when {{nihongo|[[Kamakura Yoshitarō]]|[[:ja:鎌倉芳太郎|鎌倉芳太郎]]}} approached [[Itō Chūta]] to help save the site. The pair visited Okinawa together before the prominent architect, a member of the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1897 Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law|Committee for the Preservation of Ancient Shrines and Temples]], declaring the castle the "representative work of Ryūkyūan architecture", intervened to secure an emergency designation under the [[Cultural Property (Japan)#1919 Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law|Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law]]. Demolition was halted. Itō proposed the main hall be used as the shrine haiden, thus qualifying it for funds for repair under the Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law. On 24 April 1925, as "Okinawa Jinja haiden", it was designated a [[National Treasure (Japan)#History|Specially Protected Building (National Treasure)]].{{cite web |url=http://www.museums.pref.okinawa.jp/museum/topics/detail.jsp?id=1528 |script-title=ja:「首里城を救った男」 |trans-title=The Man who Saved Shuri Castle |language=ja |publisher=[[Okinawa Prefectural Museum|Okinawa Prefectural Museum & Art Museum]] |date=1 March 2016 |accessdate=22 November 2016}} This ostensibly benign episode of heritage preservation might also be viewed as a [[:en:Postcolonialism|violent colonial "appropriation"]] of the palace of the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kings]], paradigmatic "marker of prior independence", for relocation within the "ideological universe" of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and service of the "[[:en:Empire of Japan|emperor-centred Japanese nation state]]". ==After 1945== [[File:Okinawa_Shrine2.JPG|thumb|left|250px|Okinawa Shrine on 11 September 2009]] The shrine and most of the rest of the castle were destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], and five years later, the [[:en:University of the Ryukyus|University of the Ryukyus]] was established on the former castle site in May 1950. Under [[:en:United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands|United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands]] Proclamation 16 (USCAR 16) of 1952, concerning land with uncertain ownership, the shrine grounds were transferred to the administration of [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri City]], which two years later was [[:en:Municipal mergers and dissolutions in Japan|merged]] into [[:en:Naha|Naha]].{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/site/somu/kanzai/zaicho/syoyushahumei/syoyuusyahumei.html |script-title=ja:所有者不明土地について |trans-title=Concerning land with uncertain ownership |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] |date=5 January 2016 |accessdate=26 November 2016}} In 1960, a proposal to rebuild the shrine on a plot to the east of the former castle main hall was vetoed by the University. The following year a temporary shrine was erected by the entrance to Bengadake, construction work continuing into 1962. In 1969 a tenancy agreement for the land occupied by the temporary shrine was agreed with Naha City by the Association for the Realization of the Reconstruction of Okinawa Shrine. As of 2016, Okinawa Shrine is one of eleven shrines overseen by the Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Agency, a branch of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].{{cite web |url=http://naminouegu.jp/english.html |title=Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Agency |publisher=[[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue-gū]] |accessdate=26 November 2016}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Okinawa-jinja}} * [[:en:Chōsen Jingū|Chōsen Jingū]] * [[:en:Taiwan Shrine|Taiwan Shrine]] * [[:en:Ryūkyū Kingdom|Ryūkyū Kingdom]] * [[:en:List of Cultural Properties of Japan - structures (Okinawa)|List of Cultural Properties of Japan - structures (Okinawa)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/okinawajinja.html Okinawa Jinja] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[en:Okinawa Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Okinawa-jinja}} [[id:Kuil Okinawa]] [[ja:沖縄神社]] [[zh:沖繩神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11139392}}

Kingdom |language=ja |publisher=[[:en:Shuri Castle|Shurijo Castle Park]] |accessdate=24 November 2016}} ==Dedication== [[File:Yamazaki Masatada at Okinawa Shrine.jpg|thumb|right|200px|[[:en:Masatada
